# Katie's Analysis for COMP 110 Continuous Improvement

## Idea

For this assignment I analyzed how students' reported understanding of course material affects how helpful they think pre-recorded lecture videos would be, and whether this relationship is affected by year in school. 

## Analysis

Using the data from the course survey I created 1. a bar graph of year in school vs. course understanding, 2. a scatterplot of the relationship between understanding and perceived helpfulness of pre-lecture videos, grouped by year in school and 3. a line plot of this relationship. 


### Bar graph of year in class vs. perceived course understanding. 

<img width="505" height="484" alt="image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8ba9228d-6f95-4b4d-b636-cd1065c3dfed" />

### Scatterplot of understanding vs. perceived helpfulness of pre-lecture videos, grouped by year in school 
<img width="557" height="484" alt="image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d37cd9b3-eed2-494a-878c-a57d35150936" />

### Line plot of understanding vs. perceived helpfulness of pre-lecture videos, grouped by year in school 
<img width="557" height="484" alt="image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3dda7f67-21aa-4dd2-851d-ca503e027f52" />


## Conclusions

The analysis suggests a mild negative relationship between students’ understanding of course material and how helpful they perceive optional pre-lecture videos to be. In general, students who report lower understanding seem to believe the inclusion of pre-lecture videos would be more helpful, while students with higher understanding tend to rate them slightly lower. This pattern appears relatively consistent across all student years (26–29), indicating that the relationship is not strongly dependent on year level.

Overall, the data provides some support for the idea that pre-lecture videos may be most valuable for students who feel less confident in their understanding of course material. However, the trend is not strongly pronounced so further statistical analyses would be necessary to determine if these results are significant. That said, it seems the case that all students, even those reporting greater understanding think they would be at least somewhat helpful. 

A  recommendation is to consider offering optional pre-lecture videos as a supplemental resource, particularly aimed at students who may be struggling with course content or prefer additional preparation before class. Since the resource is optional, it could provide targeted support without requiring extra time for students who already feel confident.

There are several trade-offs to consider. Creating pre-lecture videos requires additional instructional time and resources, which may increase workload for teaching staff. Additionally, not all students may use these materials, so the benefit may not be evenly distributed across the class. Some students may also rely too heavily on videos instead of engaging actively during lectures. it could be worth having ULAs make the prelecture videos to help them get more experience with teaching if that is something they are interested in, that way the students and instructors are both benefitting. 

Future improvements to this idea could include collecting more detailed data on how often students actually use pre lecture videos (not just whether they think they are helpful), or experimentally introducing videos in one section of the course and comparing performance outcomes. This would provide more evidence for whether pre lecture videos improve learning outcomes rather than just perceived usefulness.
